4 
Quality Information Document for OMI_EXTREME_STATE_GLOBAL_trend  
© EU Copernicus Marine Service – Public   
CMEMS-WAV-OMI-QUID-
EXTREME_STATE 
Issue 2.0 – November 2025 
1) INTRODUCTION 
a) Product Name  
OMI_EXTREME_STATE_GLOBAL_trend 
 
b) Indicator Evaluation Method 
 
The significant wave height (SWH), expressed in metres, is the average height of the highest 
one-third of waves. The extreme SWH is defined as the 95th percentile of the daily maximum 
of SWH over a chosen period and region. The 95th percentile (P95) represents the value 
below which 95% of the data points fall, indicating higher wave heights than usual. 
To compute this OMI, we used the reprocessed L4 SWH product, which merges between one 
and four missions at a time (WAVE_GLO_PHY_SWH_L4_MY_014_007) and is available from 
2002 to 2020.  
 
Then, following Laloue et al. [2024], three regions were selected to compute the regional time 
series: North Atlantic, South of Australia, Antarctic Circumpolar Current. Both mean and 
extreme SWH were selected on a seasonal basis to account for the seasonal variability of the 
waves (January, February and March , JFM, and July, August and September, JAS ). Daily 
data were resampled into seasonal means and P95. Both statistics are in metres. To calculate 
the 95% confidence interval on the seasonal mean and seasonal extreme time series, a 
bootstrap method was used by resampling the data and calculating the mean and extreme 
values 500 times before taking the 97.5 percentile for the upper bound and the 2.5 percentile 
for the lower bound. Finally, the trends of each time  series were then assessed by linear 
regression as in Timmermans et al. (2020) and are expressed in cm yr -1. The confidence 
interval for the trends is 90%. 
 
Finally, to produce  the global trend maps , daily data were first resampled into seasonal 
datasets, distinguishing two main seasons of the year (JFM and JAS) to capture the seasonal 
variability of wave conditions. The mean and 95th percentile of the significant wave height 
(SWH, in meters)  were then computed. Trends were derived using linear regression and are 
expressed in cm/yr. If the p -value obtained from the linear regression is less than 0.05, the 
trend is considered significant.

catalog_entry.summary: This Quality Information Document describes the evaluation method for OMI_EXTREME_STATE_GLOBAL_trend, focusing on significant wave height (SWH) and its extreme values over selected regions with seasonal variability analysis.
